nodes.name: Famous Scientists
nodes.description: Groundbreaking scientists across physics, chemistry, biology, and medicine

items.prompt: Which German-born physicist, who spent his later career at Princeton, published four groundbreaking papers in 1905, formulated the theories of special and general relativity, and derived the equation E=mc²?
items.explanation:
Born 1879 in Ulm, Germany; died 1955 in Princeton, New Jersey
Annus mirabilis (1905): published four papers on the photoelectric effect, Brownian motion, special relativity, and mass–energy equivalence
General relativity (1915) redefined gravity as the curvature of spacetime
Nobel Prize in Physics 1921 for the photoelectric effect — NOT for relativity
Fled Nazi Germany in 1933; became a U.S. citizen in 1940
Wrote a letter to FDR urging atomic research, helping launch the Manhattan Project — later called it his 'one great mistake'
Refused the presidency of Israel in 1952
Brain was removed during autopsy without family permission and studied for decades

items.prompt: Which English physicist and mathematician, author of *Principia Mathematica* (1687), formulated the three laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation, and served as Master of the Royal Mint?
items.explanation:
Born 1642 (Old Style) in Woolsthorpe, England; died 1727 in London
Three laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation laid the foundation for classical mechanics
*Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica* (1687) is one of the most important scientific works ever published
The apple story is likely apocryphal but based on Newton's own account of watching apples fall
Lucasian Professor of Mathematics at Cambridge; later Warden and Master of the Royal Mint
Co-invented calculus independently of Leibniz; the priority dispute was one of the bitterest in science history
Also made major contributions to optics: split white light with a prism and built the first reflecting telescope
Spent more time on alchemy and biblical chronology than on physics or mathematics

items.prompt: Which Polish-French physicist and chemist won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, discovered the elements polonium and radium, and coined the term 'radioactivity'?
items.explanation:
Born 1867 in Warsaw, Poland; died 1934 in Passy, France
First woman to win a Nobel Prize (Physics, 1903, shared with Pierre Curie and Henri Becquerel)
Only person to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences: Physics (1903) and Chemistry (1911)
Discovered polonium (named for Poland) and radium while processing tons of pitchblende ore by hand
Died of aplastic anemia caused by prolonged radiation exposure; her papers are still radioactive and kept in lead-lined boxes
First woman appointed professor at the Sorbonne (1906), after Pierre's death
Drove mobile X-ray units ('petites Curies') to the front lines during World War I
Her daughter Irène Joliot-Curie also won a Nobel Prize in Chemistry (1935)

items.prompt: Which English naturalist, who sailed aboard HMS Beagle to the Galápagos Islands, published *On the Origin of Species* in 1859 and proposed the theory of evolution by natural selection?
items.explanation:
Born 1809 in Shrewsbury, England; died 1882 at Down House, Kent
HMS Beagle voyage (1831–1836): studied geology, fossils, and wildlife across South America and the Galápagos Islands
*On the Origin of Species* (1859) proposed evolution by natural selection; first print run sold out in a day
Delayed publication for 20 years, partly fearing backlash — pushed to publish when Alfred Russel Wallace independently reached the same theory
Galápagos finches (later called 'Darwin's finches') showed adaptive radiation across islands
Married his first cousin Emma Wedgwood; worried about inbreeding effects on their ten children
Buried at Westminster Abbey near Isaac Newton, despite his religious doubts
Studied earthworms, barnacles, and orchids in addition to evolution — published a book on earthworms shortly before his death

items.prompt: Which Italian astronomer, sometimes called the 'father of modern science,' used an improved telescope to observe the moons of Jupiter and the phases of Venus, and was tried by the Roman Inquisition for advocating heliocentrism?
items.explanation:
Born 1564 in Pisa, Italy; died 1642 in Arcetri, near Florence
Improved the telescope (1609) and made groundbreaking observations: Jupiter's four largest moons, phases of Venus, craters on the Moon, sunspots
Championed the Copernican heliocentric model; tried by the Roman Inquisition in 1633 and forced to recant
'Eppur si muove' ('and yet it moves') — probably apocryphal, but the most famous line attributed to him
Spent his final years under house arrest; went blind before his death
The four Galilean moons of Jupiter (Io, Europa, Ganymede, Callisto) are named in his honor
Also studied motion, discovering that falling bodies accelerate uniformly regardless of mass
Died in 1642; Newton was born later that same year under the Julian calendar

items.prompt: Which Serbian-American inventor and electrical engineer developed the alternating current (AC) electrical system, the Tesla coil, and competed with Edison in the 'War of Currents'?
items.explanation:
Born 1856 in Smiljan, Austrian Empire (modern Croatia); died 1943 in New York City
Developed the AC induction motor and polyphase AC power system, which became the global standard for electricity transmission
Worked briefly for Edison before their bitter rivalry; Edison championed DC, Tesla championed AC
The tesla (T) is the SI unit of magnetic flux density, named in his honor
Patented the radio before Marconi, though Marconi received the Nobel; U.S. Supreme Court restored Tesla's patent in 1943
Died alone in Room 3327 of the Hotel New Yorker on January 7, 1943, at age 86; found by a maid two days later
FBI seized his papers after death over fears about his 'death ray' weapon designs
Famous for eccentric habits: obsession with the number 3, fear of pearls, feeding pigeons in NYC parks

items.prompt: Which Danish physicist proposed a model of the atom with electrons orbiting the nucleus in quantized energy levels, won the Nobel Prize in 1922, and later contributed to the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics?
items.explanation:
Born 1885 in Copenhagen, Denmark; died 1962 in Copenhagen
Bohr model of the atom (1913): electrons orbit the nucleus in discrete energy levels, emitting light when jumping between them
Nobel Prize in Physics 1922 for his atomic structure work and contributions to quantum theory
The Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics: a system exists in superposition until measured
Helped physicists escape Nazi-occupied Denmark; had Nobel medals belonging to von Laue and Franck dissolved in acid to hide them from the Nazis — the gold was recovered and recast after the war
His son Aage Bohr also won the Nobel Prize in Physics (1975)
Famous 1941 meeting with Heisenberg in occupied Copenhagen — the exact conversation remains a mystery, dramatized in Michael Frayn's play *Copenhagen*
Played goalkeeper for the Danish football club Akademisk Boldklub

items.prompt: Which French chemist and microbiologist developed the germ theory of disease, invented the process of heating beverages to kill bacteria (named after him), and created vaccines for rabies and anthrax?
items.explanation:
Born 1822 in Dole, France; died 1895 in Marnes-la-Coquette, near Paris
Germ theory of disease: proved that microorganisms cause fermentation and infection, overturning the theory of spontaneous generation
Pasteurization: heating liquids to kill harmful bacteria; originally developed for wine, now used primarily for milk
Created the first vaccines for rabies (1885) and anthrax (1881)
The rabies vaccine was first tested on 9-year-old Joseph Meister, who had been bitten by a rabid dog — Meister survived
'In the fields of observation, chance favors only the prepared mind' — his most famous quote
Also discovered molecular chirality (left- and right-handed crystals of tartaric acid) early in his career
The Pasteur Institute, founded in 1887 in Paris, remains one of the world's leading biomedical research centers

items.prompt: Which English theoretical physicist, diagnosed with ALS at age 21 and given two years to live, studied black hole radiation, held the Lucasian Professorship at Cambridge, and wrote the bestseller *A Brief History of Time*?
items.explanation:
Born 1942 in Oxford, England; died 2018 in Cambridge at age 76
Diagnosed with motor neuron disease (ALS) at 21; doctors gave him two years to live — he survived 55 more
Hawking radiation (1974): theoretical prediction that black holes emit radiation and eventually evaporate
*A Brief History of Time* (1988) sold over 25 million copies and spent 237 weeks on the *Sunday Times* bestseller list
Lucasian Professor of Mathematics at Cambridge (1979–2009) — the same chair held by Newton
Communicated through a speech-generating device after losing his voice; his synthesized voice became iconic
Died on March 14, 2018 — Pi Day, and the 139th anniversary of Einstein's birth
Appeared on *The Simpsons*, *Star Trek: The Next Generation*, and *The Big Bang Theory* as himself
items.cardFront: Black holes, *A Brief History of Time*, ALS

links.text: Black holes, *A Brief History of Time*, ALS

items.prompt: Which English chemist and X-ray crystallographer produced 'Photo 51,' the critical X-ray diffraction image of DNA's structure, whose data was used by Watson and Crick without her knowledge?
items.explanation:
Born 1920 in London; died 1958 in London of ovarian cancer at age 37
Photo 51 (1952): her X-ray diffraction image of DNA revealed the helical structure and key dimensions
Watson and Crick used her data without her knowledge or consent; Maurice Wilkins showed them Photo 51
Never received a Nobel Prize — the 1962 Nobel for DNA went to Watson, Crick, and Wilkins; the Nobel is not awarded posthumously
Also made major contributions to understanding the structures of RNA, viruses, coal, and graphite
Her cancer was likely caused by extensive X-ray exposure during her research
Largely written out of the DNA story for decades; Watson's memoir *The Double Helix* (1968) portrayed her dismissively
The Rosalind Franklin rover, part of the ESA ExoMars program, was named in her honor

items.prompt: Which American physicist won the 1965 Nobel Prize for quantum electrodynamics, pioneered a diagrammatic method for visualizing particle interactions, and publicly demonstrated the O-ring failure during the Challenger disaster investigation?
items.explanation:
Born 1918 in Queens, New York; died 1988 in Los Angeles of kidney cancer
Nobel Prize in Physics 1965 (shared with Schwinger and Tomonaga) for quantum electrodynamics (QED)
Feynman diagrams: intuitive visual representations of particle interactions, now ubiquitous in physics
On the Rogers Commission investigating the 1986 Challenger disaster, he famously dunked an O-ring in ice water on live TV to demonstrate the failure
Worked on the Manhattan Project at Los Alamos as a young physicist; cracked safes for amusement
*Surely You're Joking, Mr. Feynman!* (1985) became a bestselling memoir
Played bongo drums, sketched nude models, and picked locks — cultivated a reputation as a maverick
His Caltech lectures (*The Feynman Lectures on Physics*) remain among the most celebrated physics textbooks

items.prompt: Which Scottish bacteriologist accidentally discovered the world's first widely used antibiotic in 1928 when he noticed mold killing bacteria in a petri dish he had left uncovered?
items.explanation:
Born 1881 in Darvel, Ayrshire, Scotland; died 1955 in London
Discovered penicillin in September 1928 when *Penicillium notatum* mold contaminated a staphylococcus culture and killed surrounding bacteria
Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 1945, shared with Howard Florey and Ernst Boris Chain, who developed penicillin into a usable drug
Fleming's discovery was largely ignored for a decade until Florey and Chain's work in the early 1940s made mass production possible
Penicillin is estimated to have saved over 200 million lives since its introduction
Warned as early as 1945 that overuse of antibiotics would lead to resistant bacteria — a prediction now reality
Also discovered lysozyme (1923), a natural antibacterial enzyme found in tears and saliva
Served as a captain in the Royal Army Medical Corps during World War I, where he saw many soldiers die of infected wounds

items.prompt: Which Augustinian friar, working in a monastery garden in Brno, conducted experiments on pea plants that established the fundamental laws of heredity, though his work was ignored until rediscovered in 1900?
items.explanation:
Born 1822 in Hynčice, Austrian Empire (now Czech Republic); died 1884 in Brno
Conducted pea plant experiments (1856–1863) tracking traits like height, seed color, and pod shape across generations
Discovered dominant and recessive traits, the law of segregation, and the law of independent assortment
Published his results in 1866 in *Experiments on Plant Hybridization* — virtually ignored for 34 years
Rediscovered in 1900 independently by Hugo de Vries, Carl Correns, and Erich von Tschermak
An Augustinian friar who became abbot of his monastery in 1868; administrative duties consumed his later years
Studied nearly 29,000 pea plants over eight years — an enormous dataset for the era
Now called the 'father of genetics,' though he never used the word 'gene' (coined later by Wilhelm Johannsen in 1909)
items.cardFront: Pea plants, laws of heredity, Augustinian friar

links.text: Pea plants, laws of heredity, Augustinian friar

items.prompt: Which self-taught English physicist, who began his career as a bookbinder's apprentice, discovered electromagnetic induction, built the first electric motor, and invented the protective enclosure for blocking electromagnetic fields that bears his name?
items.explanation:
Born 1791 in Newington Butts, Surrey, England; died 1867 in Hampton Court
Discovered electromagnetic induction (1831): a changing magnetic field generates an electric current — the principle behind generators and transformers
Built the first electric motor (1821) and the first electric generator (dynamo)
The Faraday cage blocks external electromagnetic fields — used in electronics shielding and MRI rooms
The farad (F) is the SI unit of electrical capacitance, named in his honor
No formal education; began as a bookbinder's apprentice and educated himself by reading the books he bound
Appointed to the Royal Institution by Humphry Davy; later surpassed Davy as Britain's greatest experimental scientist
Refused a knighthood and twice declined the presidency of the Royal Society

items.prompt: Which German physicist, whose constant h is fundamental to quantum mechanics, originated quantum theory in 1900 by proposing that energy is emitted in discrete packets called quanta?
items.explanation:
Born 1858 in Kiel, Germany; died 1947 in Göttingen
Originated quantum theory (1900) by proposing that electromagnetic energy is emitted in discrete quanta, not continuously
The Planck constant (h ≈ 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s) is one of the fundamental constants of physics
Nobel Prize in Physics 1918 for his discovery of energy quanta
His son Erwin was executed by the Nazis on January 23, 1945, for involvement in the 20 July plot to assassinate Hitler
Max Planck personally wrote to Hitler pleading for Erwin's life; the plea was ignored
The Max Planck Society, successor to the Kaiser Wilhelm Society, is Germany's premier research organization with 80+ institutes
Originally a reluctant revolutionary — he introduced quanta as a mathematical trick and spent years trying to reconcile it with classical physics
items.cardFront: Quantum theory, Planck constant, energy quanta

links.text: Quantum theory, Planck constant, energy quanta

items.prompt: Which German physicist formulated the principle stating that a particle's position and momentum cannot both be precisely known simultaneously, won the 1932 Nobel Prize, and controversially led Nazi Germany's nuclear research program?
items.explanation:
Born 1901 in Würzburg, Germany; died 1976 in Munich
The uncertainty principle (1927): the more precisely you know a particle's position, the less precisely you can know its momentum, and vice versa
Nobel Prize in Physics 1932 for the creation of quantum mechanics (specifically matrix mechanics)
Headed Nazi Germany's Uranverein (uranium club) nuclear weapons program — whether he deliberately sabotaged it remains hotly debated among historians
Famous 1941 meeting with Niels Bohr in occupied Copenhagen; what was said remains a mystery and inspired the play *Copenhagen* by Michael Frayn
Walter White's alias 'Heisenberg' in *Breaking Bad* is a direct reference
After the war, was detained with other German scientists at Farm Hall in England, where their conversations were secretly recorded
Also developed the S-matrix theory and made contributions to nuclear physics and cosmic ray research

items.prompt: Which British primatologist spent decades studying wild chimpanzees at Gombe Stream in Tanzania, discovered that chimps use tools, and became a UN Messenger of Peace and global conservation advocate?
items.explanation:
Born 1934 in London; died October 1, 2025, in the United States at age 91
Began studying chimpanzees at Gombe Stream National Park, Tanzania, in 1960 — at age 26, with no university degree
Discovered that chimps make and use tools (stripping leaves from twigs to fish for termites), overturning the definition of humans as the only toolmakers
Also observed chimps hunting and eating meat, and engaging in warfare between groups — previously thought to be uniquely human behaviors
National Geographic cover story (1963) and documentary made her internationally famous
Founded the Jane Goodall Institute (1977) and the Roots & Shoots youth program
UN Messenger of Peace since 2002; spent ~300 days a year traveling and advocating for conservation
Had no formal scientific training when she started; Louis Leakey sent her to Gombe believing a fresh perspective would yield new insights
items.cardFront: Chimpanzees, Gombe, tool use in primates

links.text: Chimpanzees, Gombe, tool use in primates

items.prompt: Which two molecular biologists at Cambridge proposed the double helix structure of DNA in 1953, publishing a one-page paper in *Nature* that revolutionized biology?
items.explanation:
Watson (born 1928, American) and Crick (1916–2004, British) proposed the double helix structure of DNA in April 1953
Their one-page *Nature* paper famously ended: 'It has not escaped our notice that the specific pairing we have postulated immediately suggests a possible copying mechanism'
Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 1962, shared with Maurice Wilkins
Relied heavily on Rosalind Franklin's X-ray crystallography data (Photo 51), shown to them without her knowledge by Wilkins
Crick went on to propose the 'central dogma' of molecular biology (DNA → RNA → protein)
Watson published *The Double Helix* (1968), a controversial memoir that portrayed Franklin dismissively
Watson was stripped of honorary titles by Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory in 2019 after making racist remarks about intelligence and genetics
Crick died of colon cancer in 2004; Watson is alive as of 2026

items.prompt: Which Russian chemist organized the known elements by atomic weight into a periodic table in 1869, leaving gaps that correctly predicted the properties of elements not yet discovered?
items.explanation:
Born 1834 in Tobolsk, Siberia, Russia; died 1907 in St. Petersburg
Published his periodic table on March 6, 1869, organizing 63 known elements by atomic weight and chemical properties
Left gaps in the table and predicted the properties of undiscovered elements — gallium, scandium, and germanium were later found to match his predictions almost exactly
Legend says the arrangement came to him in a dream: 'I saw in a dream a table where all elements fell into place'
Element 101 (mendelevium, Md) is named in his honor
Not the only one working on periodicity — Lothar Meyer independently developed a similar table around the same time
Never won the Nobel Prize despite being nominated; lost by one vote in 1906, allegedly due to opposition from Svante Arrhenius
Also defined the standard for Russian vodka at 40% alcohol by volume (a persistent legend, though his actual thesis was on alcohol-water mixtures)

items.prompt: Which American astronomer and science communicator hosted the 1980 television series *Cosmos: A Personal Voyage*, championed the 'pale blue dot' photograph of Earth, and helped design the Voyager Golden Record?
items.explanation:
Born 1934 in Brooklyn, New York; died 1996 in Seattle of pneumonia, a complication of myelodysplastic syndrome
*Cosmos: A Personal Voyage* (1980) was watched by over 500 million people in 60 countries — the most-watched PBS series at the time
Persuaded NASA to turn Voyager 1 around to photograph Earth from 3.7 billion miles away, producing the 'pale blue dot' image (1990)
Helped design the Voyager Golden Record, a message from humanity sent into interstellar space
'Billions and billions' — the phrase associated with him was actually coined by Johnny Carson's parody; Sagan later embraced it as a book title
Wrote the novel *Contact* (1985), adapted into a 1997 film starring Jodie Foster
Pioneered the study of planetary atmospheres and helped explain Venus's extreme greenhouse effect
Co-founded the Planetary Society (1980), now the world's largest space-interest group
